The need to calculate for a given vector its _sum of squares_ is common in _Scientific Programming_.

If the vector is materialized an easy option is using the `np.inner()` option with itself.
It is as quick as it gets with, probably, no memory allocation.

Yet if there is a vector to materialize within an expression there are only 2 options:
1. Use `np.linalg.norm()` and square the answer.
2. Use `np.sum(np.square())` which creates an intermediate allocation.

It would be great to have `np.sum_squares()` and `np.fsum_squares()` to sum the square of each element (Where `fsum_squares()` is specialized for the non complex case).
The requirement is to be fast with no allocation.  
For instance, have this use case optimized (Ridge Regression $f \\left( \\boldsymbol{x}, \\lambda \\right) = \\frac{1}{2} {\\left\\| \\boldsymbol{A} \\boldsymbol{x} - \\boldsymbol{b} \\right\\|}_{2}^{2} + \\frac{\\lambda}{2} \\left\\| \\boldsymbol{x} \\right\\|\_{2}^{2}$):

```python
hObjFun = lambda vX, λ: 0.5 * np.fsum_squares(mA @ vX - vB) + (λ / 2.0) * np.fsum_squares(vX)
```

Alternatives:
 - Add an option in `np.linalg.norm()` to skip the last calculation in `sum(abs(x)**ord)**(1./ord)`. Namely not applying the `1./ord` operation. This is a generalization of the above for $\\left\\| \\boldsymbol{x} \\right\\|_{p}^{p}$.
 - Add support for a single input case for `np.inner()` which will apply the operation of the array with itself.

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27476","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27476/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27476/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27476/events","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27476","id":2556364512,"node_id":"I_kwDOAA3dP86YXwbg","number":27476,"title":"ENH: Add a Function to Calculate Sum of Squares of an Array","user":{"login":"RoyiAvital","id":7347975,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjczNDc5NzU=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/7347975?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/RoyiAvital","html_url":"https://github.com/RoyiAvital","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/RoyiAvital/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/RoyiAvital/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/RoyiAvital/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/RoyiAvital/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/RoyiAvital/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/RoyiAvital/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/RoyiAvital/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/RoyiAvital/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/RoyiAvital/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-09-30T11:28:26Z","updated_at":"2024-09-30T11:28:26Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Proposed new feature or change:

The need to calculate for a given vector its _sum of squares_ is common in _Scientific Programming_.

If the vector is materialized an easy option is using the `np.dot()` option with itself.
It is as quick as it gets with, probably, no memory allocation.

Yet if there is a vector to materialize within an expression there are only 2 options:
1. Use `np.linalg.norm()` and square the answer.
2. Use `np.sum(np.square())` which creates an intermediate allocation.

It would be great to have `np.sum_squares()` and `np.fsum_squares()` to sum the square of each element (Where `fsum_squares()` is specialized for the non complex case).
The requirement is to be fast with no allocation. For instance, have this use case optimized:

```python
hRidgeRegObjFun = lambda vX, λ: 0.5 * np.fsum_squares(mA @ vX - vB) + (λ / 2.0) * np.fsum_squares(vX)
```

#### Describe the workflow you want to enable
If the target is discrete multiclass, but ordinal (ordered) in nature (e.g. Likert scale, user ratings, preference levels), as opposed to nominal, would be great to have support for loss function that recognize the ordinal nature of the target variable.

(Note the difference between "learning-to-rank" and "ordinal classification". "learning-to-rank", implemented by popular boosting libraries XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost, produces relative ordering between items, and the output predictions are not class labels, while "ordinal classification" does produce class labels. Here is a blog post to introduce the reader to [loss functions for ordinal classification](http://fa.bianp.net/blog/2013/loss-functions-for-ordinal-regression/).)
#### Describe your proposed solution
All-Threshold loss is the proposed solution. [Rennie, et al](https://ttic.uchicago.edu/~nati/Publications/RennieSrebroIJCAI05.pdf) described the All-Threshold loss function. Here is an easy-to-understand blog post introducing readers to [loss functions for ordinal classification](http://fa.bianp.net/blog/2013/loss-functions-for-ordinal-regression/).
Fabian, et al implemented the [mord](https://github.com/fabianp/mord) package using logistic regression with All Threshold Loss

#### Describe alternatives you've considered, if relevant
Historically, in Logistic Regression, popular approach proposed by McCullagh ([paper](https://www.jstor.org/stable/2984952)) uses Cumulative Logit link function. Here is the main disadvantage of this approach:
> Probabilistic models for discrete ordinal response have also
been studied in the statistics literature [McCullagh, 1980;
Fu and Simpson, 2002]. However, the models suggested are
much more complex, and even just evaluating the likelihood
of a predictor is not straight-forward.
From Page 2 Rennie, Jason D. M. and Srebro, Nathan . [Loss functions for preference levels: Regression with discrete ordered labels](https://ttic.uchicago.edu/~nati/Publications/RennieSrebroIJCAI05.pdf).

Note that Cumulative Logit is the probabilistic approach, proposed by McCullagh in 1980.
All threshold loss is much easier to scale to large datasets, which is very much the purpose of HistGradientBoosting.
#### References
Papers
[1] Rennie, Jason D. M. and Srebro, Nathan, Loss functions for preference levels: Regression with discrete ordered labels.
[2] Dembczynski, Krzysztof and Kotlowski, Wojciech and Slowinski, Roman. Ordinal Classification with Decision Rules.
[3] Pedregosa, Fabian and Bach, Francis R. and Gramfort, Alexandre. On the Consistency of Ordinal Regression Methods.
[4] Li, Ling and Lin, Hsuan-Tien. Ordinal Regression by Extended Binary Classification.

Given the same inputs, the solution by MATLAB is different and more optimal then the solution of `linprog()`.  

The data are just coordinates in order to fir affine function to data with ${L}_{1}$ norm (See [Is There an L1 Norm Equivalent to Ordinary Least Squares?](https://math.stackexchange.com/questions/2846737)).

The data (`vX`, `vY`) is attached as `csv` files.

[vX.csv](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/files/13993065/vX.csv)
[vY.csv](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/files/13993067/vY.csv)


### Reproducing Code Example

```python
# vX = np.loadtxt('vX.csv') #<! Download the attached `vT.csv` file
# vY = np.loadtxt('vY.csv') #<! Download the attached `vT.csv` file

# vY = np.loadtxt('https://github.com/scipy/scipy/files/13993067/vY.csv') #<! Download the attached `vT.csv` file

polyDeg = 1
numSamples = np.size(vX)
mX = np.power(np.reshape(vX, (-1, 1)), np.arange(polyDeg + 1))
mI = -np.eye(numSamples)

vC = np.concatenate((np.zeros(polyDeg + 1), np.ones(numSamples)), axis = 0)
mA = np.concatenate((np.concatenate((mX, mI), axis = 1), np.concatenate((-mX, mI), axis = 1)), axis = 0)
vB = np.concatenate((vY, -vY), axis = 0)

sLinProgSol = sp.optimize.linprog(vC, A_ub = mA, b_ub = vB, method = 'highs')
vP = sLinProgSol.x[:(polyDeg + 1)]
```


### Error message

```shell
There is no error message, the solution is just not optimal.
```

Describe your issue.

Given the same inputs, the solution by MATLAB is different and more optimal then the solution of `linprog()`.  

The data are just coordinates in order to fir affine function to data with ${L}_{1}$ norm (See [Is There an L1 Norm Equivalent to Ordinary Least Squares?](https://math.stackexchange.com/questions/2846737)).

The data (`vX`, `vY`) is attached as `csv` files.

[vX.csv](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/files/13993065/vX.csv)
[vY.csv](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/files/13993067/vY.csv)


### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import numpy as np
import scipy as sp

# vX = np.loadtxt('vX.csv') #<! Download the attached `vT.csv` file
# vY = np.loadtxt('vY.csv') #<! Download the attached `vT.csv` file
vX = np.loadtxt('https://github.com/scipy/scipy/files/13993065/vX.csv')
vY = np.loadtxt('https://github.com/scipy/scipy/files/13993067/vY.csv')

polyDeg = 1
numSamples = np.size(vX)
mX = np.power(np.reshape(vX, (-1, 1)), np.arange(polyDeg + 1))
mI = -np.eye(numSamples)

vC = np.concatenate((np.zeros(polyDeg + 1), np.ones(numSamples)), axis = 0)
mA = np.concatenate((np.concatenate((mX, mI), axis = 1), np.concatenate((-mX, mI), axis = 1)), axis = 0)
vB = np.concatenate((vY, -vY), axis = 0)

sLinProgSol = sp.optimize.linprog(vC, A_ub = mA, b_ub = vB, method = 'highs')
vP = sLinProgSol.x[:(polyDeg + 1)]
```


### Error message

```shell
There is no error message, the solution is just not optimal.

MATLAB's solution is: 394.4629, -0.0416.
SciPy's solution is: 368, 0.

In the case above it is a great difference.

I tried other solvers (CVX, CVXOPT) which also yield the MATLAB's solution.
```

`eachrow`, `eachcol` (introduced in #29749) now return an `SliceArray` object (along with `Rows`/`Columns` aliases). The main benefit is that it will allow dispatch on the iterator to provide more efficient methods, e.g.
```
sum(A::Rows) = vec(sum(parent(A), dims=1))
```

This will encourage the use of `eachcol`/`eachrow` to resolve ambiguities in user-facing APIs, in particular, the "observations as rows vs columns" problem in the statistics/ML packages.

This also makes `eachslice` work over multiple dimensions.
